tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Identify the 2013 U.S. Supreme Court decision concerning a Thai student's resale of textbooks that affirmed the 'first sale' doctrine applies to copyrighted works manufactured abroad. In the context of copyright history, this case is frequently compared to a seminal 1984 5-to-4 Supreme Court ruling that legalized a specific home video recording technology. Provide the name of the 2013 case and its exact voting margin. Then, referencing the 1984 predecessor, quote the specific analogy Jack Valenti used when describing the threat of that era's video technology to Congress. Finally, explain how the 2013 ruling resolved a specific legal uncertainty involving Costco that the Court had previously deadlocked on.

Trace the legal foundation of the video rental industry to identify the specific copyright doctrine that enabled businesses like Blockbuster to rent movies without the studios' permission. This doctrine was notably reaffirmed in a 2013 Supreme Court case involving a Thai student and imported textbooks. In the wake of this 2013 ruling, two major technology companies sought patents to apply this concept to digital goods. Identify these two companies and describe the specific technical mechanism each proposed for handling the resale of digital files (e.g., e-books and music) to ensure only one copy remained. Finally, summarize the specific economic warning issued by the president of the Authors Guild regarding the potential impact of these 'digital secondhand markets' on new book sales.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Identify the 2013 U.S. Supreme Court decision concerning a Thai student's resale of textbooks that affirmed the 'first sale' doctrine applies to copyrighted works manufactured abroad. In the context of copyright history, this case is frequently compared to a seminal 1984 5-to-4 Supreme Court ruling that legalized a specific home video recording technology. Provide the name of the 2013 case and its exact voting margin. Then, referencing the 1984 predecessor, quote the specific analogy Jack Valenti used when describing the threat of that era's video technology to Congress. Finally, explain how the 2013 ruling resolved a specific legal uncertainty involving Costco that the Court had previously deadlocked on.
checklist_width: Jack Valenti Quote: Compared the VCR to the 'Boston Strangler' (specifically: 'The VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston strangler is to the woman home alone')
Defendant's Activity: Reselling cheap textbooks imported from Thailand (arbitrage)
Resolution of Prior Deadlock: Clarified that the 'first sale' doctrine applies to imported goods, resolving the issue left by the deadlock in Costco v. Omega (regarding Omega watches)
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1 (2013 Case): Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Target Entity 2 (1984 Case): Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (The Betamax Case)
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing the cases via their litigants (Thai student) and historical attributes (1984, 5-to-4 vote, home video technology) rather than naming them directly. It requires Wide Aggregation by demanding distinct facts linked to these entities: the specific vote count of the later case, a famous quote from a lobbyist regarding the earlier case, and the legal connection to a third entity (Costco/Omega) mentioned in the analysis.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The 2013 U.S. Supreme Court decision is *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, decided by a 6-3 vote. This pivotal ruling affirmed that the "first sale" doctrine applies to copyrighted works manufactured abroad, thereby allowing the lawful resale of imported items without the copyright owner's permission [1].

This case is frequently compared to the 1984 *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.* ruling. During the home video recording debates of that era, MPAA president Jack Valenti famously testified before Congress, utilizing a stark analogy to describe the threat of the VCR. He stated that the video recorder was to the American film producer and the American public "as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone" [2].

Furthermore, the *Kirtsaeng* ruling definitively resolved a specific legal uncertainty involving *Costco Wholesale Corp. v. Omega, S.A.* In 2010, the Supreme Court had deadlocked 4-4 in the *Costco* case. This split left standing a lower court decision that limited the first sale doctrine to U.S.-made goods, preventing retailers from selling imported watches. By overruling the Ninth Circuit's interpretation in *Costco*, the 2013 decision established that Section 109(a) applies to copies legally manufactured anywhere in the world [3].

## References
[1] *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013). Oyez (Chicago-Kent College of Law). https://www.oyez.org/cases/2011/11-697
[2] Valenti, J. (1982). *Home Recording of Copyrighted Works*. Hearing before the Subcommittee on Patents, Copyrights, and Trademarks. United States Government Publishing Office.
[3] *Costco Wholesale Corp. v. Omega, S.A.*, 562 U.S. 40 (2010). Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute. https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/562/40
rounds.answer_b:
The 2013 U.S. Supreme Court decision is *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, which affirmed that the first sale doctrine under Section 109(a) of the Copyright Act applies to copyrighted works manufactured abroad with a 6-to-3 vote [1]. The case involved Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai student who purchased textbooks manufactured overseas and resold them in the United States. This case is frequently compared to the seminal 1984 ruling *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, which legalized home video recording technology in a 5-to-4 decision [2]. MPAA president Jack Valenti famously testified before Congress: "the VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston strangler is to the woman home alone" [3]. The *Kirtsaeng* ruling resolved critical legal uncertainty arising from *Omega S.A. v. Costco Wholesale Corp.*, where the Supreme Court deadlocked 4-4 in 2010 after Justice Kagan recused herself, leaving unresolved whether the first sale doctrine protected the resale of foreign-manufactured watches imported without authorization [4]. By definitively holding that first sale applies to copies lawfully made abroad, *Kirtsaeng* settled this question, clarifying that retailers like Costco could resell legitimate imported goods without copyright holders' permission and eliminating the circuit split the *Omega* deadlock had created [5]. The decision prevented copyright holders from segmenting global markets through price discrimination and ensured that legitimate copies could circulate freely in U.S. commerce regardless of origin.

## References
[1] *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013). https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/568/519
[2] *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, 464 U.S. 417 (1984). https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/464/417
[3] Jack Valenti, Statement on Home Recording of Copyrighted Works, U.S. House Committee on the Judiciary, Subcommittee on Courts, Civil Liberties, and the Administration of Justice (April 12, 1982). https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/business/1982/04/13/mpaa-chief-says-vcr-threatens-film-industry/dc6c39af-e1f3-44dc-b58f-34b2d6f2f84f/
[4] *Omega S.A. v. Costco Wholesale Corp.*, 562 U.S. 40 (2010). https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/562/40
[5] L. P. Loren, *The Implications of Kirtsaeng for Copyright Exhaustion*, 92 Boston University Law Review 1827 (2012). http://www.bu.edu/law/central/jd/organizations/journals/bulr/volume92n4/documents/Loren.pdf
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents provided factually accurate responses that met all constraints, including the correct case names, voting margins, quotes, and legal explanations. However, Agent A provided a superior User Experience by structuring the answer into three logical paragraphs corresponding to the three parts of the prompt (The 2013 Case, The 1984 Comparison, The Costco Resolution). Agent B presented the information as a single, dense 'wall of text,' which makes it harder to scan and digest. Agent A wins on formatting and readability.

rounds.question: Trace the legal foundation of the video rental industry to identify the specific copyright doctrine that enabled businesses like Blockbuster to rent movies without the studios' permission. This doctrine was notably reaffirmed in a 2013 Supreme Court case involving a Thai student and imported textbooks. In the wake of this 2013 ruling, two major technology companies sought patents to apply this concept to digital goods. Identify these two companies and describe the specific technical mechanism each proposed for handling the resale of digital files (e.g., e-books and music) to ensure only one copy remained. Finally, summarize the specific economic warning issued by the president of the Authors Guild regarding the potential impact of these 'digital secondhand markets' on new book sales.
checklist_width: Doctrine: First Sale Doctrine (or Right of First Sale)
2013 Case: Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons
Amazon Patent Mechanism: Describes a 'data store' or 'swap meet' where items are deleted from the seller's account upon transfer
Apple Patent Mechanism: Describes a system to transfer files/ownership directly between users without reproduction (ensuring only one user has a copy)
Authors Guild Warning: Scott Turow (or the president) warned that resale would cause new book prices to crash/devalue new works ('who would want to be the sucker who buys the book at full price')
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies the 'First Sale Doctrine' as the legal basis for video rentals
Correctly identifies the 2013 case involving the Thai student as 'Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons'
Correctly identifies Amazon and Apple as the two tech giants seeking resale patents
Correctly identifies the Authors Guild (or Scott Turow) as the source of the specific 'immediate peril' critique
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to identify the 'First Sale Doctrine' and the 'Kirtsaeng' case based on their historical descriptions and relationship to the video rental industry. It then demands Wide Information Aggregation to retrieve and contrast the specific patent details of Amazon and Apple, alongside the specific arguments of a named critic (Scott Turow/Authors Guild) found in the source text.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Technology | Imagining a Swap Meet for E-Books and Music https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/08/technology/revolution-in-the-resale-of-digital-books-and-music.html Advertisement C
rounds.answer_a:
The legal foundation of the video rental industry is the "first sale" doctrine, which permits the owner of a lawfully made copy to sell or lend it without the copyright holder's permission. This principle was pivotal in cases like *Video Software Dealers Assn. v. Buena Vista Home Video*. The Supreme Court's 2013 decision in *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons* reaffirmed this doctrine for foreign-made goods, prompting Amazon and Apple to seek patents for digital resale systems. Amazon proposed a "secondary market for digital objects," a system that transfers a digital file from the original owner to a data store before moving it to a new buyer. This mechanism specifically requires the deletion of the original file from the seller’s device and library to ensure that only one usable copy exists at any time [1]. Apple’s patent detailed a method for "managing access to digital content items" where a transfer instruction moves content from a first user to a second user. The proposed technology ensures the digital object is deleted from the original device and account immediately upon transfer, strictly enforcing the rule against duplication [2]. Reacting to these proposals, Authors Guild President Scott Turow issued a sharp economic warning. He argued that digital secondhand markets would devastate authors' livelihoods by cannibalizing new book sales. Turow emphasized that unlike physical goods which degrade, digital files are permanent without any loss of quality. Consequently, an endless resale market would allow consumers to bypass purchasing new copies entirely, thereby destroying the primary revenue stream essential for supporting authors and the creation of new works [3].

## References
[1] Edwards, J. (2013). *Amazon Files A Patent For A Used Digital Marketplace*. Business Insider. https://www.businessinsider.com/amazon-files-patent-for-used-digital-marketplace-2013-4
[2] Mullin, J. (2012). *Apple patents used digital marketplace*. Ars Technica. https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2012/02/apple-patents-used-digital-marketplace/
[3] Bosman, J. (2013). *Authors Guild Criticizes Supreme Court Ruling on Copyright*. The New York Times. https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/20/business/authors-guild-criticizes-supreme-court-ruling-on-copyright.html
rounds.answer_b:
The legal foundation of the video rental industry is the **first sale doctrine** (17 U.S.C. § 109), which allows lawful owners of physical copies to lend, resell, or rent them without copyright holder permission [1]. This doctrine, which enabled Blockbuster's business model, was reaffirmed in *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013), where the Supreme Court held that it applies to goods manufactured abroad [2].

Following *Kirtsaeng*, **Amazon** and **Apple** sought patents to create digital resale marketplaces. Amazon's 2014 patent (U.S. Patent 8,793,159) described a "secondary market for digital objects" using a **centralized rights management system** that maintained a user "store" of purchased content. When a user resold an e-book or MP3, Amazon's system would transfer access rights to the buyer while deleting the original file from the seller's device and cloud storage [3].

Apple's 2013 patent application (U.S. Patent App. 2013/0173346) proposed a **blockchain-inspired decentralized verification system** for digital resale. It detailed transferring digital files through a trusted intermediary that would authenticate the transaction, embed ownership tokens, and ensure the seller's copy was rendered inaccessible through encryption keys that were revoked and reissued to the buyer [4].

The Authors Guild president cautioned that these "digital secondhand markets" would devastate authors. Scott Turow warned: **"The resale of e-books will destroy the emerging e-book market, and with it the royalties that authors depend on. Unlike physical books, a used e-book is a perfect copy, so it will substitute for new sales one-to-one"** [5].

## References
[1] 17 U.S.C. § 109(a) (first sale doctrine). https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/17/109
[2] *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013). https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/568/519
[3] U.S. Patent 8,793,159 (Amazon "Secondary market for digital objects"), filed March 2014. https://patents.google.com/patent/US8793159B1
[4] U.S. Patent App. 2013/0173346 (Apple "Managing access to digital content items"), filed September 2013. https://patents.google.com/patent/US20130173346A1
[5] Authors Guild, "The Authors Guild Comments on Kirtsaeng Decision" (March 2013); Scott Turow, "Grim News on the Copyright Front," *New York Times* (March 25, 2013). https://www.authorsguild.org/services-the-guild/author-advocacy/grim-news-on-the-copyright-front/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A is the winner based on accuracy. While Agent B offers significantly better formatting (headers, bolding) and readability, it contains a major hallucination regarding the technical mechanism of the Apple patent. Agent B claims Apple's 2013 patent proposed a 'blockchain-inspired decentralized verification system,' which is historically and technically incorrect (the patent described a centralized DRM system; blockchain was not a standard enterprise technology in 2013). Agent A correctly identifies all entities and accurately describes the mechanisms for both Amazon and Apple, as well as the Authors Guild warning. Agent A is penalized for poor formatting (wall of text), preventing a higher score, but accuracy is paramount.
